content:
Let (M,1,⋅)(M,1,\cdot)(M,1,⋅) be a monoid, and let E=(MM,id,∘)E = (M^M, \text{id}, \circ)E=(MM,id,∘) be the monoid of endomorphisms on the carrier MMM (not necessarily monoid homomorphisms). I'm interested in the following EEE-set:

G:Set→E-SetG : \mathbf{Set} \to E\text{-}\mathbf{Set}G:Set→E-Set
U(GX)=M×XU(GX) = M \times XU(GX)=M×X
e⋅(m,x)=(e(m),x)e \cdot (m, x) = (e(m), x)e⋅(m,x)=(e(m),x)

Notice, GGG behaves kind of like the free MMM-set functor F:Set→M-SetF : \mathbf{Set} \to M\text{-}\mathbf{Set}F:Set→M-Set, but it extends the action to all endomorphisms eee, not just those of the form m′⋅(−)m' \cdot (-)m′⋅(−) (which, of course, forms forms a monoid homomorphism φ:(M,1,⋅)→(MM,id,∘)\varphi : (M,1,\cdot) \to (M^M, \text{id}, \circ)φ:(M,1,⋅)→(MM,id,∘) (defined by φ(m)=m⋅(−)\varphi(m) = m \cdot (-)φ(m)=m⋅(−)).

Using this φ\varphiφ, we get a functor φ∗:E-Set→M-Set\varphi^* : E\text{-}\mathbf{Set} \to M\text{-}\mathbf{Set}φ∗:E-Set→M-Set. If we consider GGG as a functor mapping into the [[full image]] of φ∗\varphi^*φ∗, then U:FullImage(φ∗)→SetU : \text{FullImage}(\varphi^*) \to \mathbf{Set}U:FullImage(φ∗)→Set is the right adjoint to G:Set→FullImage(φ∗)G : \mathbf{Set} \to \text{FullImage}(\varphi^*)G:Set→FullImage(φ∗)! (The induced monad is the free MMM-set monad.)

My question is as follows: is this GGG "well-known", or easy to construct out of FFF (or the free EEE-set) somehow? I know there's lots of structure available (e.g., φ!\varphi_!φ!​ and φ∗\varphi_*φ∗​), but it's not clear to me how to assemble this into GGG.

(I came up with this construction "manually", so maybe there's something cleaner! Really, all I want is a way to decompose the monad M×(−):Set→SetM \times (-) : \mathbf{Set} \to \mathbf{Set}M×(−):Set→Set in such a way that the left adjoint lands in a category where the objects come equipped with (M→M)(M \to M)(M→M)-actions.)

(Another remark: I proved that this works given

  • any monoid E=(N,1,⋅)E = (N, 1, \cdot)E=(N,1,⋅)
  • a monoid homomorphism φ:(M,1,⋅)→(N,1,⋅)\varphi : (M, 1, \cdot) \to (N, 1, \cdot)φ:(M,1,⋅)→(N,1,⋅)
  • an NNN-action structure on MMM, α:N×M→M\alpha : N \times M \to Mα:N×M→M
  • such that α(φ(c),1)=c\alpha (\varphi(c), 1) = cα(φ(c),1)=c.
    My construction/proof uses all of this structure, although maybe there's another approach that uses less structure. It would even be helpful to me if the above structure or something similar shows up/has a common name...)

Btw, a minor notational thing: mathematicians use +, 0 for commutative monoids and ⋅\cdot⋅, 1 for not-necessarily-commutative monoids, so seeing all these +'s and 0's gets the wrong part of my brain to turn on.

(Sorry, good point - edited! The construction I'm presenting works for any monoid on MMM; although, I'm mainly interested in commutative monoids MMM anyway, so if there's some way to answer my question that uses commutativity somehow, I'd be perfectly happy.)

So let's see if I understand this. You're taking a monoid MMM and a set XXX and getting MMM^MMM (by which you mean the monoid of endomorphisms of MMM, not the monoid of all functions from MMM to MMM) to act on M×XM \times XM×X in the obvious way. And you're wanting a slick way to show that there's a functor from Set\mathsf{Set}Set to MMM^MMM-Set\textsf{Set}Set that sends any set XXX to M×XM \times XM×X, made into an MMM^MMM-set this way.

I'm just trying to restate your question in a more informal way.
Yes, exactly! (And, I'd like that functor to have a right adjoint.)
Oops, race condition - I do care about all functions MMM to MMM (not just monoid homomorphisms)! (Although if restricting to monoid homomorphisms helps somehow, that would be interesting to know.)
(For a concrete example, let let (M,1,⋅)=(N,0,+)(M,1,\cdot) = (\mathbb{N},0,+)(M,1,⋅)=(N,0,+); I'm interested in applying e=(n↦⌈nlog⁡n⌉)e = (n \mapsto \lceil n \log n \rceil)e=(n↦⌈nlogn⌉).)

Okay, all functions. In that case you're really just using the fact that MMM^MMM is a monoid that acts on MMM. So we might change your question to this more general one where we've got a monoid AAA that acts on a set SSS.

I've got a monoid AAA that acts on a set SSS. Then for any set XXX, AAA acts on S×XS \times XS×X in the obvious way. Can we show that there's a functor from Set\mathsf{Set}Set to AAA-Set\textsf{Set}Set that sends any set XXX to S×XS \times XS×X, made into an AAA-set in this way, and does this functor have a right adjoint?
This is more general so a priori less likely to be true, but I don't see how taking A=MMA = M^MA=MM, S=MS = MS=M helps, so I'd be more inclined to think about this more general version.

Yes, this is a great framing, thanks. Although, I'm willing to land in a category with AAA-sets as objects but possibly more restrictive homomorphisms (this [[full image]] idea).

Given just the action, I can define the functor Set→A-Set\mathbf{Set} \to A\text{-}\mathbf{Set}Set→A-Set.

To define a right adjoint, my idea was to restrict the functor to Set→FullImage(φ∗)\mathbf{Set} \to \text{FullImage}(\varphi^*)Set→FullImage(φ∗) (the objects are AAA-sets but the morphisms are morphisms of SSS-sets), where φ\varphiφ is a monoid homomorphism from SSS (which now needs a monoid structure) to AAA.

Then, I'm able to define a right adjoint when I also assume that φ(s)⋅1=s\varphi(s) \cdot 1 = sφ(s)⋅1=s (which, admittedly, feels like it comes out of nowhere).

This FullImage\text{FullImage}FullImage thing feels wrong to me; I have to go to work now, and let someone else pick up the baton now and carry it further, but given the functor from Set\mathsf{Set}Set to AAA-Set\mathsf{Set}Set that we're talking about, if it has a right adjoint it should be fairly simple to describe this right adjoint directly and check that it's a right adjoint straight from the definition of "adjoint". If that works, there are also people here who might be able to solve the problem in a slicker way.

Sounds good, thanks for the advice! I'd be happy to avoid the full image as well, although so far that's the only solution that I've found that gives me a right adjoint inducing the S×(−)S \times (-)S×(−) monad*.

*I do need to assume a monoid structure on SSS for this to even be a monad...

I'm reminded of this result, which might be relevant:

This is from "Monoid Properties as Invariants of Toposes of Monoid Actions" by Jens Hemelaer and Morgan Rogers.

Yes, that could imply my guess if we specialize by taking NNN to be the trivial monoid.

In case it helps Harrison: Psh(M)\mathsf{Psh}(M)Psh(M) is a funky name for the category of MopM^{\text{op}}Mop-sets, where MopM^{\text{op}}Mop is the monoid MMM with the reversed multiplication

x⋅opy:=yxx \cdot_{\text{op}} y := y x x⋅op​y:=yx

An MopM^{\text{op}}Mop-set is also known as a right MMM-set. If you don't like this you can say the AAA in my comment was secretly MopM^{\text{op}}Mop. (We are now using MMM in a different way than in your original question.)

So, the key idea is that we get the right adjoint by using HomN\text{Hom}_NHomN​. If you don't know what that means, I'm sure @David Egolf (he/him) would be happy to explain. :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:

Yes, this sounds quite reasonable... although maybe with N=MMN = M^MN=MM and B=MB = MB=M or something? Wondering if I can then horizontally compose the adjunctions, where my GGG is FFF (free MMM-set) followed by (−)⊗MM(-) \otimes_M M(−)⊗M​M or something... (pondering on scratch paper now)
Oh, yes, I think this is exactly the right thing!!

Letting my N=MMN = M^MN=MM, I get that

  • FX⊗MM=(FX×M)/∼≅FXFX \otimes_M M = {(FX \times M)/\sim} \cong FXFX⊗M​M=(FX×M)/∼≅FX (which makes sense - M=F1M = F1M=F1)
  • U(HomN(M,A))={f:M→UA∣f(m′⋅m)=m′⋅f(m)}U(\text{Hom}_N(M, A)) = \{f : M \to UA \mid f(m' \cdot m) = m' \cdot f(m)\}U(HomN​(M,A))={f:M→UA∣f(m′⋅m)=m′⋅f(m)}, which is the same as UAUAUA when M=NM = \mathbb{N}M=N (the only nontrivial choice is f(0)f(0)f(0))
This is fantastic, thanks so much!!! What a great paper (link), thanks @David Egolf (he/him)!
Ohh, although, I suppose it's simpler to do what @John Baez suggests (M≜1M \triangleq 1M≜1, N≜MMN \triangleq M^MN≜MM, and B≜MB \triangleq MB≜M), indeed, and the same argument applies. :)
(oh, duh, because M=F1M = F1M=F1!)
Yes, this is looking good. And this theorem should be fairly easy to prove, or at least convince yourself of, since there are explicit "formulas" for both functors in the adjunction.
Indeed - in retrospect, it feels like it was staring me in the face!

(After equipping a set SSS with an AAA-action, just define the left adjoint FX=X⋊SFX = X \rtimes SFX=X⋊S, the copower of XXX with SSS; and then UA=Hom(S,A)UA = \text{Hom}(S, A)UA=Hom(S,A), which is a set. And they're obviously adjoint, because this is the definition of copower! :man_facepalming:)
Well, at some point you have to check that these functors exist, and that they're adjoint.
Thanks for dusting off our paper @David Egolf (he/him) :smile_cat:

Upon further thought, this may not be exactly what I'm looking for... the right adjoint is R(A)=Hom(S,A)={f:S→UA∣∀a:M,f(m⋅s)=m⋅f(s)}R(A) = \text{Hom}(S, A) = \{ f : S \to UA \mid \forall a : M, f(m \cdot s) = m \cdot f(s) \}R(A)=Hom(S,A)={f:S→UA∣∀a:M,f(m⋅s)=m⋅f(s)}, not what I wrote above. This seems rather bad - with L(X)=X⋊SL(X) = X \rtimes SL(X)=X⋊S, I get R(L(X))={(f1,f2):S→X×S∣∀m:M,f1(m⋅s)=f1(s)∧f2(m⋅s)=m⋅f2(s)}R(L(X)) = \{ (f_1, f_2) : S \to X \times S \mid \forall m : M, f_1(m \cdot s) = f_1(s) \land f_2(m \cdot s) = m \cdot f_2(s) \}R(L(X))={(f1​,f2​):S→X×S∣∀m:M,f1​(m⋅s)=f1​(s)∧f2​(m⋅s)=m⋅f2​(s)}. When S=NS = \mathbb{N}S=N and M=NNM = \mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N}M=NN, this R(L(X))R(L(X))R(L(X)) is just XXX, since f1(s)=f1(s+0)=f1(0)f_1(s) = f_1(s + 0) = f_1(0)f1​(s)=f1​(s+0)=f1​(0) and f2f_2f2​ has to be the identity function. But I wanted R(L(X))=S×XR(L(X)) = S \times XR(L(X))=S×X...

Seemingly relatedly, for my application, I do want a map M×RA→RAM \times RA \to RAM×RA→RA that gives me an MMM-action for everything in the image of RRR. When I chose RRR to be UUU (using my "full image" idea), this was immediate, but for R(A)=Hom(S,A)R(A) = \text{Hom}(S, A)R(A)=Hom(S,A), this isn't the case...

Since the full image idea does work but just feels a bit ad-hoc, maybe I should frame the question differently. The full image category on φ∗\varphi^*φ∗ (given a monoid homomorphism φ:M→N\varphi : M \to Nφ:M→N) is a full subcategory of Psh(N)\mathbf{Psh}(N)Psh(N). Sorry, right - I was writing the symbols to record my thoughts, but maybe it's not so human-readable. :) Maybe the following message is the better one for reading - I want a map M×RA→RAM \times RA \to RAM×RA→RA for whatever the right adjoint RRR is.

With my full image idea, this is immediate, since RARARA just gets the carrier of AAA (what I've been writing UAUAUA). But with RA=Hom(S,A)RA = \text{Hom}(S, A)RA=Hom(S,A), I no longer have this (unless the monoid is commutative to begin with or something, which I certainly don't want to assume).

The full-subcategory idea "smells right" to me for my application - I'm really working in type theory, and it would make lots of sense if my construction was just a reflector/modality applied to the free NNN-set...

Yesterday I thought I had pierced the veil and figured out what you were trying to do. Now I no longer know what you're trying to do, and I'd probably only understand it if you gave a complete new description, not just corrections like "I used to want this, and now I want this".

Other readers, younger and more focused on what you're doing, may not have my problem!

I also thought what you suggested was the right solution, but realized upon studying this adjunction more that it's not quite what I'm looking for. Let me try to give a fresh description of the problem:

I have two monoids, MMM and NNN, along with a monoid homomorphism φ:M→N\varphi : M \to Nφ:M→N. (Typically for me, M=NM = \mathbb{N}M=N with addition and NNN is arbitrary functions N→N\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}N→N with composition.)

I'm interested in viewing the free MMM-set as an NNN-set, which I can do since (in my cases of interest) I have an NNN-action structure on MMM, written ⋅:N×M→M{\cdot} : N \times M \to M⋅:N×M→M. I'm working in a type theory whose semantics lets me choose a pair of adjoint functors F⊣U:C→SetF \dashv U : \mathcal{C} \to \mathbf{Set}F⊣U:C→Set, and I want it to be the case that objects of C\mathcal{C}C come equipped with an NNN-action, viewable through UUU as maps N×UA→UAN \times UA \to UAN×UA→UA for all A:CA : \mathcal{C}A:C.

Because of this last condition (which I was so used to when I posed my earlier question that I didn't even think to mention it, sorry!), it seems like I should choose my category C\mathcal{C}C to have objects being NNN-sets and let UUU genuinely extract the carrier of an NNN-set.

If I let C\mathcal{C}C be the category of NNN-sets, though, the left adjoint to UUU will be the free NNN-set, but I only care about the free MMM-set! And for type theory-esque reasons, I can only pick a single adjunction. So, my idea is to let C\mathcal{C}C have objects be NNN-sets, but have a morphism from A→BA \to BA→B be a morphism of MMM-sets from φ∗(A)→φ∗(B)\varphi^*(A) \to \varphi^*(B)φ∗(A)→φ∗(B) (this is the [[full image]] category of φ∗\varphi^*φ∗).

With this in mind, I'm set. The free MMM-set functor Set→C\mathbf{Set} \to \mathcal{C}Set→C is genuinely left adjoint to UUU: since the maps of C\mathcal{C}C only preserve MMM-set structure and thus the "free-ness" is successfully only with respect to the MMM-set structure, the notion of free is what I want, M×XM \times XM×X. To prove this, I only need the mildly odd condition connecting the monoid homomorphism φ\varphiφ and the action ⋅\cdot⋅ that φ(m)⋅1=m\varphi(m) \cdot 1 = mφ(m)⋅1=m.

The question is, though, whether this can be recovered out of more elementary parts. Set\mathbf{Set}Set, Psh(M)\mathbf{Psh}(M)Psh(M), and Psh(N)\mathbf{Psh}(N)Psh(N) all have tons of structure: there are

  • a free-forgetful adjunction F⊣U:Psh(M)→SetF \dashv U : \mathbf{Psh}(M) \to \mathbf{Set}F⊣U:Psh(M)→Set,
  • similarly F⊣U:Psh(N)→SetF \dashv U : \mathbf{Psh}(N) \to \mathbf{Set}F⊣U:Psh(N)→Set,
  • an adjoint triple φ!⊣φ∗⊣φ∗:Psh(M)→Psh(N)\varphi_! \dashv \varphi^* \dashv \varphi_* : \mathbf{Psh}(M) \to \mathbf{Psh}(N)φ!​⊣φ∗⊣φ∗​:Psh(M)→Psh(N),
  • the adjunction we discussed yesterday (−)⋊M⊣Hom(M,−):Psh(N)→Set(-) \rtimes M \dashv \text{Hom}(M, -) : \mathbf{Psh}(N) \to \mathbf{Set}(−)⋊M⊣Hom(M,−):Psh(N)→Set,
  • and more!
    So, I'd kind of expect my adjunction to decompose into the composite of some known adjunctions or something.

My best guess (based on domain-specific intuition) for what might happen is that the inclusion functor ι\iotaι from the full image category might have a left adjoint LLL:

Harrison Grodin said:

Is it possible that the inclusion has a left adjoint LLL? If so, then my original adjunction will just be the following composite of adjunctions:

That would be great, since it would render this "full image" business as just the category of algebras for the idempotent monad ι∘L:Psh(M)→Psh(M)\iota \circ L : \mathbf{Psh}(M) \to \mathbf{Psh}(M)ι∘L:Psh(M)→Psh(M). And then my construction would just be L∘FL \circ FL∘F, applying this elusive LLL to the free MMM-set in such a way that its carrier remains M×XM \times XM×X.

So: I'd be very happy if I could find this left adjoint LLL! If that doesn't exist, maybe this construction I'm interested in can come out of some other composite of adjoints or something, but I feel less certain about where to look...
(Thanks for going through this, btw - I appreciate your time and thoughts!)

I think this is the adjunction you're describing, but it will indeed not be the case that the right adjoint is the forgetful functor (I think). I think your "full image" category is just equivalent to PSh(M)?

I think that's the adjunction I was looking at here:
Harrison Grodin said:

But I'm no longer so sure it's the right thing. In my calculation, I mistakenly computed the right adjoint; I should've said:

U(HomN(M,A))={f:M→UA∣∀m,n,f(n⋅m)=n⋅f(m)U(\text{Hom}_N(M, A)) = \{ f : M \to UA \mid \forall m,n, f(n \cdot m) = n \cdot f(m)U(HomN​(M,A))={f:M→UA∣∀m,n,f(n⋅m)=n⋅f(m)

This doesn't quite seem to be what I'm looking for, though - given my typical choice of N=MMN = M^MN=MM, I get

U(HomN(M,FX⊗MM))=U(HomN(M,FX))={f:M→M×X∣∀m:M,n:MM,f(n(m))=n(f(m))}=XU(\text{Hom}_N(M, FX \otimes_M M)) = U(\text{Hom}_N(M, FX)) = \{ f : M \to M \times X \mid \forall m : M, n : M^M, f(n(m)) = n(f(m)) \} = XU(HomN​(M,FX⊗M​M))=U(HomN​(M,FX))={f:M→M×X∣∀m:M,n:MM,f(n(m))=n(f(m))}=X

even though I want R(LX)=M×XR(LX) = M \times XR(LX)=M×X.

I'm not sure I see why Psh(M)\mathbf{Psh}(M)Psh(M) should be the same as my full image category, but I may be mistaken! Psh(M)\mathbf{Psh}(M)Psh(M) has

  • objects MMM-sets and
  • morphisms MMM-action-preserving maps

but FullImage(φ∗)\text{FullImage}(\varphi^*)FullImage(φ∗) has

  • objects $$N$-sets and
  • morphisms MMM-action-preserving maps (using φ∗\varphi^*φ∗).

So I think FullImage(φ∗)\text{FullImage}(\varphi^*)FullImage(φ∗) is a full subcategory of Psh(M)\mathbf{Psh}(M)Psh(M), but not the same?
But you pointed out that every M-set can be made into an N-set, which would make the functor essentially surjective, so an equivalence

My understanding of the situation is this:

  • We have monoids MMM and NNN, and a monoid morphim φ:M→N\varphi:M \to Nφ:M→N
  • Any (left) NNN-set induces a corresponding (left) MMM-set via precomposition with φ\varphiφ. Indeed, we can precompose a functor FN:N→SetF_N:N \to \mathsf{Set}FN​:N→Set with φ:M→N\varphi:M \to Nφ:M→N to get a functor :M→Set:M \to \mathsf{Set}:M→Set.
  • We are interested in the MMM-sets that can be produced in this way. I think we would like to know: are there any MMM-sets which can't be obtained in this way (via precomposition with φ\varphiφ)?
For an easy example suppose N=1; then we only get M-sets where M acts trivially.

That's a nice example!

I think very often we won't be able to get all the MMM-sets in this way. Intuitively, φ\varphiφ can send mmm to an element φ(m)\varphi(m)φ(m) which satisfies more equations than mmm does. So the ways in which φ(m)\varphi(m)φ(m) can act will potentially be more constrained than the ways in which mmm can act.

I'd say "has more properties", not just "obeys more equation". For example, let ϕ:N→Z\phi: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}ϕ:N→Z be the usual inclusion of additive monoids. Then an N\mathbb{N}N-set is just a set with a map from it to itself, but from ϕ\phiϕ we only get those where this map is invertible.
The inclusion of the integers in the rationals is even more interesting.

We've now observed we don't get all MMM-sets in this way - great!

If I'm correctly understanding what @Harrison Grodin wants, I think one goal is to understand the resulting full subcategory CCC of [M,Set][M, \mathsf{Set}][M,Set], obtained by keeping only the objects we can get by precomposing NNN-sets with φ:M→N\varphi:M \to Nφ:M→N. In particular, I think @Harrison Grodin wants to know if there is a left adjoint to the inclusion functor :C→[M,Set]:C \to [M, \mathsf{Set}]:C→[M,Set].

To take an example, we might let NNN be the monoid 111 (having just the identity element). Then every NNN-set has NNN acting trivially, and we can only obtain (via precomposition with φ\varphiφ) the MMM-sets where MMM acts trivially.

Keeping only such MMM-sets, we obtain a full subcategory of [M,Set][M, \mathsf{Set}][M,Set]. Does the inclusion of this subcategory to [M,Set][M, \mathsf{Set}][M,Set] admit a left adjoint?

Let me try to re-express your question using more words and fewer symbols. There's an inclusion of the category of trivial MMM-sets (i.e. the category of sets) into the category of MMM-sets. Does this have a left adjoint?

There are two obvious functors from the category of MMM-sets to the category of sets: the one sending an MMM-set XXX to its underlying set, and the one sending XXX to the subset of XXX on which MMM acts trivially.

If we have a homomorphism φ:M→N\varphi: M \to Nφ:M→N, the surjective-injective factorization of the homomorphism induces the hyperconnected-localic factorization of the corresponding geometric morphism (that was also in the paper that David pointed to earlier). In less topos-theoretic language, φ∗\varphi^*φ∗ will already be fully faithful when φ\varphiφ is surjective, so the full image will coincide with PSh(N). So indeed the left adjoint you're looking for exists (it's φ!\varphi_!φ!​).

However, Harrison has said a few times that he's interested in a case where φ\varphiφ is injective AND there is an action ψ:N×M→M\psi: N \times M \to Mψ:N×M→M which (although Harrison didn't say this explicitly, so correct me if I'm wrong) satisfies ψ∘(φ×id)=μ\psi \circ (\varphi \times \mathrm{id})=\muψ∘(φ×id)=μ, the multiplication on MMM. In that case, for any MMM-set AAA, we can define an NNN-action on AAA via (n,a)↦ψ(n,1)⋅a(n,a) \mapsto \psi(n,1)\cdot a(n,a)↦ψ(n,1)⋅a, and this will restrict along φ\varphiφ to the original action on AAA, making the full image the whole category.

Responding to John's question, φ!\varphi_!φ!​ is neither of those functors: it's instead the functor sending an MMM-set to it's set of connected components (which can also be expressed A↦A⊗M1A\mapsto A \otimes_M 1A↦A⊗M​1)

Finally I should mention the general injective case, to cover the N→Z\N \to \ZN→Z example: when the geometric morphism is localic, every object of the domain topos is a sub-quotient of one coming from the inverse image functor. Without the topos jargon: every MMM-set will be a sub-MMM-set of a quotient-MMM-set of φ∗(A)\varphi^*(A)φ∗(A) for some NNN-set AAA. It's fun to think about how this is the case for the homomorphism N→Z\N \to \ZN→Z :)

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

However, Harrison has said a few times that he's interested in a case where φ\varphiφ is injective AND there is an action ψ:N×M→M\psi: N \times M \to Mψ:N×M→M which (although Harrison didn't say this explicitly, so correct me if I'm wrong) satisfies ψ∘(φ×id)=μ\psi \circ (\varphi \times \mathrm{id})=\muψ∘(φ×id)=μ, the multiplication on MMM.

Yes, ψ\psiψ does satisfy this condition!! It's inter-derivable with the condition ψ∘(φ×1)=id\psi \circ (\varphi \times 1) = \text{id}ψ∘(φ×1)=id I gave (where 1:1→M1 : 1 \to M1:1→M is the unit of the monoid MMM).

In that case, for any MMM-set AAA, we can define an NNN-action on AAA via (n,a)↦ψ(n,1)⋅a(n,a) \mapsto \psi(n,1)\cdot a(n,a)↦ψ(n,1)⋅a, and this will restrict along φ\varphiφ to the original action on AAA, making the full image the whole category.

However, I don't think ψ(n,1)⋅1\psi(n, 1) \cdot 1ψ(n,1)⋅1 is an action in the case I'm interested in, since ψ\psiψ is not a homomorphism in the MMM component. When M=NM = \mathbb{N}M=N with addition, N=MMN = M^MN=MM with composition, and ψ\psiψ is function application, I don't have that (n∘n′)(0)=n(0)+n′(0)(n \circ n')(0) = n(0) + n'(0)(n∘n′)(0)=n(0)+n′(0). (Otherwise, I agree with you!) So I'm still not sure that the full image is the whole category...

Aha good point! So my observation would be accurate if you were dealing with the endomorphisms of M as a monoid but not as a set. But we can factor M→NM \to NM→N as M→Mon(M,M)→NM \to \mathsf{Mon}(M,M) \to NM→Mon(M,M)→N, where the first part will have essentially surjective full image and the latter may be easier to compute the full image from?
